summary: The article provides illustrative evidence of the capital-intensive nature of the AI economy and the state's constitutive role in building digital infrastructure, supporting the theory's critique of the 'techno-feudalism' thesis.

claim_level_implications.why: The report of massive expansion in computing capacity (30% annually) and smart computing power (43% growth) illustrates that AI dominance requires significant physical infrastructure investment, contradicting the image of platforms as passive rentiers.
evidence_from_document: overall computing capacity expanding by about 30 per cent annually
smart computing power is projected to grow by 43 per cent this year
claim_level_implications.proposed_revision: Add a footnote or illustrative example noting that state-led AI initiatives (e.g., China's national computing network) demonstrate the continued necessity of massive physical capital expenditure in the digital economy.

claim_level_implications.why: The creation of a national computing network and the state-led coordination of provinces to optimize resource allocation directly illustrates the state's constitutive role in shaping the digital economy, challenging the idea that sovereignty has been 'parcelized' among private platforms.
evidence_from_document: Beijing is knitting together a national computing network... designed to match business demand with underused resources across regions
10 provinces and municipalities... had joined a unified platform
claim_level_implications.proposed_revision: Use this as a case study to strengthen the argument that tech dominance is underwritten by state industrial policy and geopolitical strategy rather than private neo-feudal usurpation.

recommended_follow_up: Investigate if similar levels of physical infrastructure investment are being mirrored by private US-based AI firms to further strengthen C1.
